summary: Critical multi-stage attack detected on vm3-forwarder: Initial brute force attack from 192.168.100.99 against Administrator account, followed by successful account creation ('hacker'), privilege escalation to administrators group, and establishment of multiple persistence mechanisms (scheduled task 'backdoor', registry run key). Additional encoded PowerShell execution and privilege enumeration commands observed. All malicious activity traced to bash session (PID 1643, user socadmin). System also generated numerous false positives related to legitimate GPG operations, system services, and cron jobs. Attack appears to be simulated via logger commands but follows realistic attack pattern requiring immediate response.

incidents.threat: Multi-Stage Attack Chain: Brute Force → Privilege Escalation → Persistence
incidents.mitre_tactic: TA0006 - Credential Access, TA0004 - Privilege Escalation, TA0003 - Persistence
incidents.mitre_technique: T1110.001 - Password Guessing, T1078.003 - Local Accounts, T1053.005 - Scheduled Task, T1547.001 - Registry Run Keys
incidents.recommended_action: IMMEDIATE: 1) Isolate vm3-forwarder from network; 2) Block source IP 192.168.100.99 at firewall; 3) Reset Administrator account password; 4) Remove user 'hacker' and review all local admin group membership; 5) Audit scheduled tasks and registry run keys for 'backdoor' entries and remove; 6) Conduct forensic analysis of bash session (PID 1643, user socadmin) for additional IOCs; 7) Review all authentication logs for successful logins from 192.168.100.99; 8) Hunt for encoded PowerShell command execution artifacts
incidents.fp_reasoning: Multiple chained attack stages from single source IP with clear malicious intent (brute force → account creation → privilege escalation → persistence mechanisms). All events executed via logger commands from same bash session, indicating simulated or real attack scenario rather than legitimate activity.

incidents.recommended_action: 1) Decode base64 payload 'JABjAGwAaQBlAG4AdA' to determine full command intent; 2) Review all PowerShell execution logs for related activity; 3) Check for network connections initiated around event timestamp; 4) If C2 communication identified, isolate host and initiate incident response; 5) Review parent process bash (PID 1643) for additional suspicious commands
incidents.fp_reasoning: Base64-encoded PowerShell commands are frequently used to obfuscate malicious payloads and evade detection. Executed from same compromised session as other attack activity (INC-001). Legitimate administrative PowerShell typically not encoded.

incidents.recommended_action: 1) Correlate with INC-001 - part of same attack chain from 192.168.100.99; 2) Review all commands executed by socadmin user during session; 3) Determine if socadmin account is compromised or being used as attack vector; 4) Audit all privilege changes and group modifications; 5) Review access logs for lateral movement attempts; 6) Consider full password reset for all local accounts
incidents.fp_reasoning: Privilege enumeration ('whoami /priv') combined with account manipulation commands (net user/net localgroup) indicates reconnaissance phase of attack. Timing and source correlation with brute force and privilege escalation events confirms malicious intent.

incidents.threat: Benign System Operations - GPG and Service Management
incidents.recommended_action: No immediate action required. These appear to be legitimate system operations: 1) GPG operations for fwupd (firmware update daemon) signature verification; 2) Normal systemd service lifecycle events; 3) Routine update-notifier operations; 4) PCI bridge configuration warnings (hardware-related, not security). Continue monitoring for anomalies.
incidents.fp_reasoning: Events flagged by DR-007 rule appear to be false positives. GPG operations are legitimate fwupd processes running as root for firmware signature verification. PCI bridge warnings are benign kernel hardware enumeration messages. Systemd service start/stop events for update-notifier are routine maintenance. No indicators of malicious service manipulation. Rule may need tuning to reduce noise from legitimate system operations.

incidents.recommended_action: No immediate action required. Events represent normal cron daemon operations: 1) Scheduled debian-sa1 system activity reporting; 2) Standard cron daemon lifecycle (start/stop/reboot); 3) Hourly cron job execution. Post-incident: Review cron jobs for any unauthorized entries, particularly after account compromise in INC-001.
incidents.fp_reasoning: All events are legitimate scheduled task operations on Linux system. debian-sa1 is standard Debian system activity reporter. Cron daemon starts, stops, and @reboot execution are normal system behavior. However, given the confirmed compromise in INC-001, cron persistence should be audited to ensure no malicious jobs were installed during the attack.
